questions.stem: Screening: how do you map OSI vs TCP/IP when asked what practitioners actually implement?
options: TCP/IP is the deployed model; OSI is a teaching reference with finer splits
OSI's seven layers are what every stack ships; TCP/IP is only theory
They have identical layer counts and names always
TCP/IP removes IP and keeps only Ethernet frames
questions.explanation: Real stacks follow TCP/IP; OSI helps discuss roles like session/presentation.

questions.stem: TCP vs UDP: what one-line tradeoff do you give in a screening interview?
options: UDP always retransmits lost packets like TCP
TCP is reliable and ordered; UDP is lightweight with no delivery guarantees
TCP never uses ports; UDP always does congestion control
They differ only in encryption, not reliability
questions.explanation: Choose TCP for reliability; UDP when you want low overhead or custom control.

questions.stem: Explain the TCP three-way handshake steps an interviewer expects you to name.
options: SYN, then data, then FIN with no ACKs
Only ACK and FIN in either order
SYN, SYN-ACK, then ACK to agree on sequence state
Four identical SYNs from both sides simultaneously
questions.explanation: Both sides exchange initial sequence numbers before application data.

questions.stem: Flow control vs congestion control: how do you keep them distinct in an interview?
options: They are the same algorithm with two names
Congestion only limits the receiver window size
Flow control only runs inside routers, never endpoints
Flow protects the receiver; congestion protects the network path
questions.explanation: Receiver window vs path capacity; TCP reacts to both signals.

questions.stem: HTTP vs HTTPS: what do you say HTTPS adds at a high level for security?
options: A different application verb set with no security change
TLS encryption and server authentication under HTTP semantics
Only compression; encryption is handled by IP alone
UDP instead of TCP for all web traffic
questions.explanation: HTTPS is HTTP over TLS; confidentiality and integrity for the session.

questions.stem: DNS role: what problem does it solve that users never type as numbers?
options: Encrypts every packet between browser and origin
Routes packets at layer 2 using MAC learning only
Maps human names to IP addresses (and related records)
Replaces TCP congestion control on the public internet
questions.explanation: DNS resolves names to addresses so clients know where to connect.

questions.stem: CDN high-level: what do you claim it primarily optimizes for end users and origins?
options: Serving cached content from edge nodes closer to users
Replacing DNS entirely with anycast-only routing
Making origin databases strongly consistent worldwide
Eliminating the need for TLS on static assets
questions.explanation: Edges cut latency and origin load by caching near the audience.

questions.stem: IP address vs MAC address: which layer story do you tell in a networking screen?
options: MAC is end-to-end across the internet; IP is only on the LAN
They are interchangeable identifiers at every hop
MAC replaces IP after the first router always
IP is logical/network routing; MAC is link-local hardware addressing
questions.explanation: IP survives across hops; MAC is rewritten per link as frames are forwarded.

questions.stem: Routing vs switching: what contrast lands well in a networking screening round?
options: Routers only learn MAC tables; switches compute BGP paths
They are identical operations with different marketing names
Switching forwards on L2/MAC within a LAN; routing forwards on L3/IP between networks
Switching always needs global internet topology knowledge
questions.explanation: Switches bridge a broadcast domain; routers interconnect networks.

questions.stem: NAT: what problem does it solve for typical home and office IPv4 networks?
options: Encrypts DNS queries by default on every resolver
Lets many private hosts share scarce public IPv4 addresses
Replaces ARP with a global name service
Guarantees end-to-end IP transparency for every peer
questions.explanation: NAT translates private addresses/ports onto a shared public endpoint.

questions.stem: TLS handshake idea: what must happen before application data can be considered safe?
options: Peers authenticate (usually server) and agree on keys/ciphers
Only a TCP SYN is needed; TLS adds nothing before data
The client emails a password in cleartext once
Routers negotiate AES keys hop-by-hop instead
questions.explanation: Handshake establishes identity and session secrets, then encrypts records.

questions.stem: Idempotent HTTP methods: which claim matches REST interview expectations?
options: POST is always idempotent; GET never is
GET, PUT, and DELETE are typically treated as idempotent; POST often is not
No HTTP method is ever idempotent by definition
Only TRACE is idempotent; others mutate twice on retry
questions.explanation: Safe retries matter; POST usually creates anew unless designed carefully.

questions.stem: Latency vs bandwidth: how do you explain why a fat pipe can still feel slow?
options: Bandwidth alone fixes RTT for tiny request/response chats
Latency and bandwidth are the same measurement in milliseconds
High bandwidth helps bulk transfer; high RTT still hurts chatty round trips
Only bandwidth matters for DNS; latency never does
questions.explanation: Many small RPCs are RTT-bound; large downloads are more bandwidth-bound.

questions.stem: What does ARP resolve when a host needs to send on the local Ethernet?
options: Domain names to certificate fingerprints
TCP ports to process IDs on the local machine
BGP AS numbers to geographic regions
IPv4 addresses on the LAN to MAC addresses
questions.explanation: ARP (or ND for IPv6) finds the next-hop link-layer address.

questions.stem: What is a port number used for at the transport layer when many apps share one host?
options: Choosing which wire color to use on the switch
Multiplexing connections so many apps share one IP
Encrypting payloads instead of TLS
Replacing the IP header on every hop
questions.explanation: Ports demux traffic to the correct socket/application on a host.

questions.stem: Stateful firewall idea: why track connection state beyond simple IP allowlists?
options: Firewalls never inspect ports or protocols
State tracking disables NAT by design
To allow return traffic for established flows while blocking unsolicited inbound
Only MAC filtering is needed for internet edge security
questions.explanation: Conntrack lets replies through for sessions the inside initiated.

questions.stem: What does a default gateway do for a host that needs to leave its local LAN?
options: Forwards packets destined outside the local subnet
Resolves all DNS names without contacting any server
Assigns MAC addresses to every new NIC forever
Terminates TLS for every outbound HTTPS request
questions.explanation: Off-subnet traffic is sent to the gateway for L3 forwarding.

questions.stem: MTU and fragmentation: what practical advice do you give about large packets?
options: MTU only applies to UDP and never to TCP
Fragmentation always improves latency on the internet
Ethernet MTU is irrelevant once you use IPv6
Bigger than path MTU can fragment or black-hole; path MTU discovery matters
questions.explanation: Oversized packets fail or fragment; PMTUD avoids silent drops when possible.

questions.stem: What is DHCP's job when a laptop first joins a network and needs connectivity?
options: Encrypt the Wi-Fi radio with WPA keys only
Compile BGP routes for the entire internet
Lease IP, gateway, DNS, and related config automatically
Replace HTTP with a binary RPC protocol
questions.explanation: DHCP hands out addressing and common network parameters on join.

questions.stem: Keep-alive / persistent HTTP: what benefit do you cite versus new TCP each request?
options: Removes the need for DNS forever after the first hit
Avoids repeated handshakes and warm-up for subsequent requests
Guarantees zero latency regardless of geography
Makes UDP mandatory for all static assets
questions.explanation: Reusing connections amortizes TCP/TLS setup cost across requests.

questions.stem: TCP slow start: what intuition do you share about why cwnd grows carefully at first?
options: To probe available capacity without dumping a huge burst into an unknown path
Because receivers cannot advertise a window until slow start ends
Slow start disables ACKs until the transfer finishes
It only applies to UDP video streams
questions.explanation: Exponential probe finds bandwidth; loss/signals then enter congestion avoidance.

questions.stem: Head-of-line blocking in HTTP/1.1 pipelining vs HTTP/2: what do you contrast?
options: HTTP/1.1 never had any form of HOL concern
HTTP/2 multiplexing reduces TCP-level HOL for concurrent streams on one connection
HTTP/2 removes TCP entirely so HOL cannot exist
Pipelining eliminated all blocking including TCP loss recovery
questions.explanation: H2 streams share one TCP; packet loss can still stall all streams (QUIC helps).

questions.stem: DNS caching and TTLs: what failure mode appears when TTLs are very long?
options: Long TTLs always improve consistency of every record instantly
TTL only affects TLS certificates, never DNS answers
Clients may keep stale IPs after backend moves or failovers
Caches ignore TTL and refresh every packet
questions.explanation: Long TTL cuts query load but slows propagation of changes.

questions.stem: Anycast CDN: how does a single IP reach different edges for different users?
options: Each client runs a VPN to a random POP by hashing cookies
Anycast rewrites MAC addresses into global unicast IPs
DNS is disabled; ARP floods the whole internet instead
Routing advertises the same prefix from many locations; users hit nearby announcements
questions.explanation: BGP anycast steers traffic to the topologically closest advertising site.

questions.stem: TLS certificate validation: what do you check beyond 'padlock present'?
options: Only that the cipher suite string contains AES
Chain to a trusted CA, name match, and not-expired/revocation policy as applicable
That the server IP equals the certificate serial number
Validation is optional if HTTP status is 200
questions.explanation: Identity rests on PKI checks, not merely that encryption happened.

questions.stem: Nagle's algorithm vs delayed ACK: what latency pitfall do you warn about?
options: Nagle always reduces latency for tiny messages
Delayed ACKs only affect UDP checksums
Their interaction can delay small writes; often disable Nagle for chatty RPCs
They only matter on localhost loopback interfaces
questions.explanation: Small write + wait-for-ACK patterns can stall; tune for interactive traffic.

questions.stem: SYN flood: what is the attack idea and a classic mitigation direction?
options: Overwhelm half-open state; SYN cookies or better backlog defenses help
Flood only FIN packets so handshake never starts
Corrupt DNSSEC signatures on every resolver
Force all clients onto UDP port 53 exclusively
questions.explanation: Attackers exhaust connection state; cookies avoid storing full state early.

questions.stem: ECN and modern congestion signals: what do you say beyond 'drop = congestion'?
options: ECN replaces TCP with a new transport protocol
Routers must encrypt ECN bits end-to-end with TLS
ECN only works on token-ring networks historically
Marked packets can signal congestion without loss when endpoints honor ECN
questions.explanation: Explicit marks let senders back off before queues overflow into loss.

questions.stem: Split horizon / NAT hairpin: why can two internal hosts fail to reach each other via the public VIP?
options: Internal hosts cannot use DNS by definition
TCP forbids two private addresses on one LAN
Hairpin NAT may be missing so public-destined packets never loop correctly
VIPs only work for UDP traceroute packets
questions.explanation: Need hairpin/NAT loopback or split-horizon DNS to internal addresses.

questions.stem: HTTP idempotency keys for POST: when would you introduce them mid-interview?
options: Only for GET caching at the CDN layer
When clients may retry create requests and you must avoid duplicate side effects
When TLS is disabled and you need checksums
Idempotency keys replace status codes entirely
questions.explanation: Safe retries of non-idempotent APIs need server-side deduplication.

questions.stem: BGP at a high level: what decision does it make that OSPF inside an AS does not?
options: Policy-rich routing between autonomous systems on the internet
Learning MAC addresses on a single Ethernet switch
Assigning DHCP leases to home routers
Terminating TLS sessions at the edge POP
questions.explanation: BGP exchanges reachability across ASes with administrative policy.

questions.stem: Connection migration / QUIC pitch: what problem with TCP+TLS middleboxes do you cite?
options: QUIC removes encryption so middleboxes can rewrite freely
QUIC can survive some path changes better; TCP 4-tuple identity is brittle
TCP already migrates connections without any handshake state
QUIC only runs over ICMP echo requests
questions.explanation: Connection IDs help when IP/port change; encrypted transport limits ossification.

questions.stem: Load balancer L4 vs L7: what tradeoff do you articulate when picking a tier?
options: L7 is always faster than L4 for every workload
L4 can terminate TLS certificates but L7 cannot
L4 is cheap/fast on tuples; L7 can route on HTTP content at higher cost
They are identical; only the brand name differs
questions.explanation: Choose L4 for throughput; L7 when you need host/path/header awareness.

questions.stem: TIME_WAIT accumulation: why do short-lived many connections hurt a server?
options: TIME_WAIT means the NIC hardware failed permanently
Only clients enter TIME_WAIT; servers never do
It increases MTU until fragmentation stops
Sockets linger in TIME_WAIT, consuming ports/state after close
questions.explanation: Rapid connect/close churn exhausts ephemeral resources; reuse or tune carefully.

questions.stem: DNSSEC vs DNS over HTTPS: what different threats do these two mechanisms address?
options: They are the same protocol with two marketing names
DNSSEC authenticates DNS data; DoH encrypts the client-resolver channel
DoH signs zone data; DNSSEC only encrypts queries
Neither relates to integrity or privacy of lookups
questions.explanation: DNSSEC = authenticity of answers; DoH/DoT = privacy to the resolver.

questions.stem: Bufferbloat: how do you describe the latency symptom when queues grow under load?
options: Buffers that are too small always maximize goodput with no downside
Bufferbloat only occurs on satellite links historically
Oversized queues add delay even when bandwidth remains available
It means ARP tables overflow on the access switch
questions.explanation: Bloated queues raise RTT; AQM/FQ helps keep latency low.

questions.stem: Same-origin policy vs CORS: what do you say the browser is actually enforcing here?
options: Scripts cannot freely read cross-origin responses unless CORS allows it
CORS replaces TLS for all cross-site images
Same-origin only applies to WebSocket ping frames
Browsers never block cross-origin XHR by default
questions.explanation: SOP isolates origins; CORS is the explicit server opt-in for sharing.

questions.stem: Path asymmetry and NAT timeouts: why can long-idle TCP die mysteriously?
options: TCP forbids idle connections longer than one RTT
Asymmetry only affects UDP traceroute, never TCP
NAT never tracks ports for TCP sessions
Middlebox state expires; keepalives or shorter idle help detect/refresh
questions.explanation: Stateful NAT/firewalls drop silent flows; apps need heartbeats.

questions.stem: HTTP caching: Cache-Control vs ETag—how do you assign roles in an interview answer?
options: ETag sets max-age; Cache-Control only signs certificates
Neither is used once a CDN is present
Cache-Control sets freshness policy; ETag validates whether content changed
ETags encrypt the response body end-to-end
questions.explanation: Freshness avoids revalidation; validators enable cheap 304 responses.

questions.stem: IPv6 neighbor discovery vs ARP: what parallel do you draw for link-layer resolution?
options: IPv6 removes the need for any link-layer address resolution
ND resolves IPv6 next-hop link addresses using ICMPv6 instead of ARP
ARP is reused unchanged for all IPv6 deployments
ND only assigns global BGP communities
questions.explanation: Same job as ARP—map IP to MAC—but via ICMPv6 ND messages.

questions.stem: Senior tradeoff: terminate TLS at the load balancer vs end-to-end to the app—what do you weigh?
options: Edge termination eases cert ops/inspection but trusts the internal hop; E2E preserves stronger trust boundaries
Edge termination always removes the need for any certificates
End-to-end TLS makes HTTP/2 multiplexing impossible
They are identical for threat model and operational cost
questions.explanation: Balance observability and ops against lateral-trust and compliance needs.

questions.stem: You debug multi-second p99 with healthy bandwidth: what senior checklist do you start with?
options: Only increase NIC speed; latency cannot come from protocols
RTT, DNS, TLS handshake reuse, HOL/loss, and app-level chatty round trips
Disable all congestion control to maximize p99
Assume MAC address conflicts are the only cause
questions.explanation: Tail latency is often round-trip and setup bound, not raw capacity.

questions.stem: Designing retries across a fleet: what thundering-herd pitfall do you call out?
options: Immediate full-rate retries always heal faster with no downside
Retries are unnecessary if you use UDP
Synchronized retries amplify outages; use jittered exponential backoff
Backoff only matters for DNS, never for HTTP APIs
questions.explanation: Uncoordinated synchronized clients can DDoS your own recovery path.

questions.stem: CGNAT and peer-to-peer: why is 'just open a listening socket' naive on mobile networks?
options: Mobile networks assign globally routable IPs to every handset always
TCP cannot traverse any NAT by protocol design
P2P only fails when DNSSEC is enabled
Carrier NAT blocks unsolicited inbound; need hole-punching or relays (e.g., TURN)
questions.explanation: Inbound reachability dies behind CGNAT; ICE/STUN/TURN are the real toolkit.

questions.stem: Zero-downtime deploy behind a LB: what connection-draining concern do you raise?
options: Killing all sockets instantly is fine if DNS TTL is under one day
Stop new traffic, finish in-flight, then tear down—or clients see resets mid-request
Load balancers never hold long-lived connections
Draining only applies to UDP syslog, never HTTP
questions.explanation: Graceful deregistration prevents cut connections during rolling deploys.

questions.stem: mTLS between services: what attack surface shrinks, and what ops cost appears?
options: mTLS removes the need for authorization checks entirely
Certificates never expire in service meshes
Stronger service identity; you must manage cert issuance/rotation everywhere
mTLS only encrypts DNS, not application bytes
questions.explanation: Workload identity improves East-West trust; PKI lifecycle becomes critical.

questions.stem: Anycast + stateful TCP: what senior risk do you flag for long-lived connections?
options: Route changes can move flows to a different PoP that lacks connection state
Anycast guarantees sticky TCP state across the whole internet forever
BGP withdrawals never affect established sockets
Stateful services should always bind only to 127.0.0.1 publicly
questions.explanation: Anycast is great for UDP/stateless edges; TCP needs careful stickiness design.

questions.stem: Observability: why is RED/USE at the network edge incomplete without DNS and TLS timings?
options: DNS and TLS never appear in real user monitoring
Only packet loss metrics matter for web performance
TTFB excludes all protocol overhead by definition
Name lookup and handshake often dominate TTFB before the first byte of HTTP
questions.explanation: Break down connect/TLS/DNS phases or you misattribute slowness to the app.

questions.stem: Rate limiting at L7 vs L3/L4: when do you insist on application-aware limits?
options: IP limits always equal user fairness on shared NATs
L3 limits can read HTTP bodies without termination
When abuse is per-user/API-key/costly endpoint, not just per source IP
Application limits replace the need for any WAF or auth
questions.explanation: CGNAT and bots share IPs; identity-aware quotas match business risk.

questions.stem: CAP-ish networking reality: strong consistency across regions vs low latency—what do you say?
options: Bandwidth alone removes the speed-of-light constraint
Cross-region sync adds RTT; many systems choose locality and accept weaker freshness
Anycast makes every write instantly visible worldwide
TCP retransmission eliminates the need for distributed consensus
questions.explanation: Physics and quorum protocols trade latency for consistency; design explicitly.
